results.title: 2025 (10) TMI 776
results.snippet: HC affirmed the ITAT and dismissed the appeals, holding that orders of the CIT(A)-11 issued after 18.06.2018 were erroneous, illegal and without jurisdiction. DGIT (Investigation) Karnataka & Goa had supervisory/administrative jurisdiction over CIT(A)-11 and had requested explanation and a halt to further orders; nonetheless CIT(A)-11 decided numerous disparate appeals shortly thereafter. Given that history and the confirmed findings in earlier rounds, the HC found no error in the ITAT's setting aside of those CIT(A)-11 orders.

results.title: 2025 (5) TMI 892
results.snippet: Reopening an assessment for the relevant year was invalid because the Assessing Officer relied on the same search-and-seizure materials and appraisal report that underlay the original assessment; that reliance amounted to a mere change of opinion, not fresh material or undisclosed income, and therefore notice under Section 148/147 was unlawful and was quashed. The notice was not time barred because amended limitation rules permit reassessment beyond four up to six years; prior sanction by the senior tax officer was valid under the pre amendment law; and a notice digitally signed on 31.03.2021 remained within time despite portal upload on 01.04.2021. Writ allowed and impugned orders set aside.

results.title: 2025 (5) TMI 629
results.snippet: The Bombay HC remanded the matter to CIT(A) regarding withholding tax liability on payments for transponder services under India-USA DTAA. The court found that lower authorities failed to analyze whether services constituted "process" or "secret process" under royalty provisions, rendering their orders non-speaking. The HC directed CIT(A) to verify: (i) if final determination exists that service provider was not liable for tax, then no withholding tax liability; (ii) payments made before Finance Act 2012 cannot attract retrospective withholding tax liability; (iii) for post-2012 payments, examine agreements to determine if payments constitute royalty under domestic law or treaty, applying beneficial provisions under Section 90(2).

results.title: 2025 (4) TMI 1092
results.snippet: MP HC upheld Tribunal's decision allowing coal mining company's deductions for education, community development, sports/recreation, and social welfare expenses as revenue expenditure, finding these were mandatory under National Coal Wage Agreement rather than voluntary. Environmental expenses and LPG/medical camp expenses were remanded for re-examination. Additional depreciation was allowed for increased production. However, HC reversed Tribunal's ruling on overburden removal expenses, holding these constitute capital expenditure regardless of mining stage, as they represent mine development/extension rather than operational mining costs.

results.title: 2025 (3) TMI 412
results.snippet: Estimation of income was contested on account of alleged bogus purchases; the tribunal had made additions based on unproved purchases and a 25% uplift was sustained by the appellate authority. The court found that some supplies were supported by delivery challans and valid documents while others lacked credible documentation; stereotyped supplier affidavits were rightly rejected. The absence of reliable documentary evidence and the implausible explanations justified upholding additions and concurrent findings of fact, and no substantial question of law warranted interference with the authorities' decisions.

results.title: 2024 (10) TMI 1209
results.snippet: Rent receipts from letting properties must be characterised by reference to the assessee's principal business activity; where the memorandum of association shows letting as the main object, rent is business income and not income from house property. The controlling legal test compares the nature of the activity and the company's main objective, not mere ownership of land or leases. Applying that test, rent derived from leased properties that constitute the assessee's principal business is assessable under profits and gains of business; accordingly the prior classification as income from house property was erroneous and reversed in favour of the assessee.

results.title: 2024 (8) TMI 886
results.snippet: The HC held that no additions can be made under Section 153A in completed or unabated assessments if no incriminating material is found during search under Section 132 or requisition under Section 132A, following the SC ruling in Principal Commissioner of Income-tax v. Abhisar Buildwell. However, completed or unabated assessments may be reopened under Sections 147/148 if conditions prescribed therein are met. The court declined to decide on the revenue's question of law, leaving all related contentions open.
